text: The surgeon aligns the resected sections of the intestine, resembling two rosebuds, using interrupted sutures to create a tension-free anastomosis of the back wall.

The surgeon performs an indocyanine green test to assess tissue perfusion, followed by placing two interrupted sutures in the center of the anastomosis using ovicryl. A transition stitch is then placed at the corner to secure the junction.

The surgeon uses a technique where sutures are thrown and delivered to the hand, pulling up on the suture with the left hand. At the corner, a locking stitch is placed to prevent purse string formation, followed by another stitch before starting from the opposite side.
The surgeon sets the needle in place and takes another stitch, using small motions with the left hand to maintain efficient movement during the suturing process.
The surgeon performs a transition stitch to facilitate forehand suturing, ensuring proper tension and loop formation in the jejunostomy procedure.

The surgeon uses a loaded needle with the top of the curve on the front side to create tension, setting the suture with the needle tip. The technique involves meeting the last suture with the first suture from the corner, ensuring full thickness bites.

The surgeon ties the suture, ensuring it sits smoothly, and places protective lumbers to relieve tension from the staple or suture line. More lumbers are used than usual due to the longer length of the line.
The surgeon closes the remaining part of the jejunostomy that was not addressed by the tinea, placing a final suture at the corner. A leak test is performed following the closure.
